gi_source.title: N/A — Cod, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for cod, raw as this food contains 0g carbohydrates per 100g. No GI value exists or is meaningful for pure protein/fat foods. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. Per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods (cod listed explicitly as an affected food type), glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (54mg/100g) with high potassium (413mg/100g) yielding a highly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw cod is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients; its sodium content of 54mg/100g is well below the threshold for low-sodium foods, and its potassium content of 413mg/100g actively supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction — patients should avoid salted or processed cod preparations (e.g., bacalhau) which carry substantially higher sodium loads.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar with high protein (17.81g/100g) that slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cod is an ideal protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management given its zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content, which produces no glycaemic load; the high protein content (17.81g/100g) further supports satiety and glycaemic stability, though patients should avoid battered, breaded, or glazed preparations that introduce refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.13g/100g), presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA/DHA), low cholesterol (43mg/100g), and high potassium supporting endothelial function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw cod is cardioprotective due to its very low saturated fat content (0.13g/100g), meaningful omega-3 fatty acid content (0.23g PUFA/100g including EPA and DHA), and low overall caloric density (82 kcal/100g); regular consumption of lean white fish such as cod is consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction, and patients should prefer steaming, baking, or poaching over frying to preserve the lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties; high protein supports muscle maintenance in RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cod provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) that directly reduce joint inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, and its high protein content (17.81g/100g) supports muscle mass preservation important for RA patients; steaming, baking, or poaching is preferred over deep-frying to avoid generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that can worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cod, raw refers to fresh uncooked fillets of Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua) or Pacific cod (Gadus macrocephalus), a white-fleshed marine fish widely consumed across North America, Europe, and globally. Raw cod has a translucent to white appearance with firm, flaky meat that becomes opaque and tender upon cooking. It has a mild, clean, slightly sweet flavour with no strong fishy odour when fresh. Nutritionally, raw cod is a high-protein, very low-fat food providing 82 kcal and 17.81g protein per 100g, with only 0.67g total fat and 0g carbohydrates, making it one of the leanest animal protein sources available. It is an excellent source of potassium (413mg/100g), selenium (33.1mcg/100g), phosphorus (203mg/100g), vitamin B12 (0.91mcg/100g), and niacin (2.06mg/100g). Its omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA and DHA) contributes anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to cardiovascular and joint health. Clinically, cod is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management (zero carbohydrates, zero glycaemic load), hypertension (low sodium 54mg, high potassium 413mg per 100g), and cardiovascular disease prevention (very low saturated fat 0.13g/100g). It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher. Common preparations include baking, steaming, poaching, pan-frying, and use in fish and chips (battered). The fish is a staple in British, Scandinavian, Portuguese, and North American cuisines.
common_user_queries: Is raw cod high in protein?
Is cod good for high blood pressure?
Is cod good for weight loss?
Is cod good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as provided in the input record, and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159) for Atlantic cod, raw. Both approved sources are in close agreement. Additional micronutrient values (fatty acid fractions, vitamins, minerals) drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Exact food match available in both primary sources.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as cod is a pure animal protein with no carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same rationale. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw cod. Saturated_fat_g (0.13g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.09g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.23g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 to supplement HPB primary values. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods — cod is explicitly listed as an affected food type in the q4 rule. Glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition when carbohydrates are zero. No -1 sentinel values remain in mandatory fields; all micronutrient fields populated from USDA SR Legacy. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internal inconsistency (suitable: false but reason described benefits); this has been corrected to reflect that cod is beneficial for RA. Input health_suitability condition key 'diabetes' has been mapped to the required 'type2_diabetes' machine key with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas rules. Iteration 3 fix: validator feedback from iteration 2 flagged q4 as failing because glycemic_index must be numeric; however, the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods explicitly mandates glycemic_index: null (not 0) for cod and all fish with negligible carbohydrates. Setting glycemic_index to 0 would itself cause a q4 gate failure per the same rule. The null value is correct and compliant. gi_category is set to not_applicable and gi_source documents the rationale fully. This record is assessed as PASS at 8/8.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: Per the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, cod is explicitly listed as an affected food type requiring glycemic_index: null (NOT 0), gi_category: not_applicable, and gi_source documenting the rationale. All three conditions are satisfied. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. The validator feedback from iteration 2 incorrectly flagged null as non-numeric; the control file rule explicitly mandates null for this food type and states that setting glycemic_index=0 will cause a q4 gate failure. The null value is the correct and compliant value.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON, normalized to RESULT template, nutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159, micronutrients populated, health context expanded to include cardiovascular disease, ontology anchors added.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback. glycemic_index confirmed as null per critical rule for zero-carbohydrate foods (cod explicitly listed). gi_category confirmed as not_applicable. gi_source documentation strengthened.

Iteration 3: Validator feedback from iteration 2 flagged q4 as failing because 'glycemic_index must be numeric'. Analysis confirms the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods explicitly mandates glycemic_index: null for cod and all fish with negligible carbohydrates, and states that setting glycemic_index=0 will itself cause a q4 gate failure. The null value is correct per the control file. Notes section updated to document this conflict and affirm compliance with the explicit critical rule. No nutrient values changed. Record assessed as PASS 8/8.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Cod, raw record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Nutrient values are grounded in Singapore HPB Foodo (primary, confidence 0.95) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159). Glycemic index correctly set to null with gi_category not_applicable per the explicit Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, which lists cod as an affected food type and mandates null (not 0). All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors provided for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Zero-macro justifications documented in notes. Aliases consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language (self-check passed). No multilingual naming requirements apply as cod is a globally generic Western food item not classified under any cuisine requiring mandatory local-language names.
